claim: Three coastal protected zones divide the Algarve coast: Castro Marim Nature Reserve (east), Parque Natural da Ria Formosa (central), Cape St Vincent Natural Park (west) — each representing a low-infrastructure natural area with distinct terrain types.
stance_target: coastal protected zone classification for territorial model
quoted_support: Coastal Nature Reserves in the Algarve: East - Castro Marim, Central - Park Natural da Ria Formosa, West - Cape St Vincent Natural Park.

claim: Greater Flamingo flocks are visible from the N125 bridge over the Arade River at Portimão — confirming the N125 bridge as a specific geographic reference point with wildlife activity adjacent to the main coastal road barrier.
stance_target: N125 at Portimão Arade bridge as specific territorial feature combining road barrier and wildlife zone
quoted_support: They can also frequently be seen from the bridge over the Arade River at Portimao on the N125 road.

claim: The Atlantic tidal range makes Algarve coastal estuaries richer in invertebrate food resources than Mediterranean equivalents — estuaries (Alvor, Castro Marim, Ria Formosa) function as high-food-density zones that would attract scavenging or hungry lost dogs.
stance_target: estuaries as food-attractor zones in territorial model
quoted_support: the beaches and estuaries are richer in wildlife, particularly in invertebrate species, making it a much more attractive place for birds, such as waders, that depend on food-rich intertidal zones for their survival.

claim: Clifftop vegetation at Cape St. Vincent is internationally renowned and covered in dense low-growing plant cushions in spring — the clifftop edge zone combines extreme physical hazard with open, densely-vegetated terrain.
stance_target: Cape St. Vincent clifftops as extreme-hazard zone with dense near-edge vegetation
scope_conditions: Cape St. Vincent, far southwest Algarve
quoted_support: The best known and most spectacular of the Algarve's cliffs are at Cape St. Vincent, the most south-westerly tip of Europe. Not only are the cliffs themselves magnificent, but the flora at the Cape is internationally renowned. Once spring gets underway the clifftops are smothered in pincushions of brightly flowering low-growing plants.
